title: 1.1: Identifies the function that constructs the system command argv for SSH exec (e.g., getSystemCommand in sftpd/ssh_cmd.go)
1.2: Explains how the destination path is normalized before being resolved (e.g., quote trimming, forcing leading slash, path.Clean)
1.3: Reports that the destination argument passed to the OS process is a filesystem resolved absolute path under the user home directory (e.g., /tmp/.../home/dest)
1.4: Identifies the rsync specific safety options that can be injected into argv (e.g., --safe-links, --munge-links)
1.5: Identifies the permission condition that determines which safety option is injected (e.g., create_symlinks permission selects between --safe-links and --munge-links)

1.6: Reports observed argv for a normal looking invocation under a permission context where the server injects the expected safety option (e.g., --safe-links prepended when user has create_symlinks permission)

1.7:  Reports observed argv for an adversarial looking invocation under a different permission context attempting to confuse option handling (e.g., client supplies both --safe-links and --munge-links with restricted permissions)
1.8: Shows that the stub received the same argv that was built by the code path (e.g., BUILT_ARGV matches STUB_CAPTURE)
1.9: Identifies the execution mechanism as direct exec rather than shell invocation (e.g., exec.Command in Go)
1.10: Shows metacharacters preserved as literals in captured argv proving no shell interpretation (e.g., semicolon survives as part of a single argument)

1.11: States the security implication of the observed argv behavior regarding privilege boundary with evidence-backed reasoning (e.g., concludes that conflicting flags behavior is a potential weakness but requires understanding rsync semantics to determine exploitability)
